title: The harness, not the model, decides production agent reliability
summary: Two teams running agents in production converge on the same lesson: the harness around the model — durable state, guardrails, evals — decides reliability, because the model is the interchangeable part.
takeaways: Spend your engineering on the harness — durable state, guardrails, evals — because the model is the interchangeable part and the plumbing is what keeps you in production.
Roughly 80% of production agent code is reliability, observability, and durable-state plumbing, not business logic — move it to the platform layer.
Encode recurring PR corrections as deterministic checks (Semgrep rules, architectural tests) the agent runs itself, so review stops burning out senior engineers.

sources.title: Grid Dynamics x Temporal — agentic platform in production
Xavier's Florian Bautz — guardrails for the code-review bottleneck

body:
## What happened

If your coding agent works in a demo but falls over in production, the gap is almost never the model. On 2026-06-11, Grid Dynamics CTO Eugene Steinberg and Temporal CTO Maxim Fateev published [a conversation on building an enterprise agentic platform](https://youtube.com/watch?v=-0Ly0cjPdjM) with a blunt number: roughly **80 percent of production agent code** is reliability, observability, retries, queues, and durable state — not the business logic anyone set out to write. Grid Dynamics, an engineering partner for Fortune 1000 companies, adopted Temporal's durable execution after hitting production outages while scaling a FinTech agentic platform. Their framing splits the agent (the loop of conversation history, LLM calls, tool calls) from the **harness** — the surrounding rules, tools, context management, sandboxing, and guardrails. Steinberg's argument: harness quality often matters more than model choice, since models are interchangeable while harnesses are not.

## Why it matters

The wall you hit is the last mile. Your agent provably works; then you have to make it survive crashes, flaky APIs, mainframes, and multi-day waits without losing state — and that work dwarfs the agent itself. A second practitioner reports the same from a different angle. Florian Bautz, an AI engineer at Xavier, argues in [a discussion on the code-review bottleneck](https://youtube.com/watch?v=W1uG25of2t0) that the same identical model "succeeds or fails across harnesses," and that as teams generate far more code, human review burns out senior engineers. Two teams, two problems, one conclusion: spend on the harness.

## How it works

1. **Split agent from harness.** Treat the loop as small; put reliability, retries, and durable state in the [platform layer, not the app](https://youtube.com/watch?v=-0Ly0cjPdjM).
2. **Make state durable.** Port the prototype to durable execution so a crash or deploy resumes instead of restarting.
3. **Encode review as checks.** Turn recurring PR (proposed-change) corrections into [Semgrep rules and architectural unit tests](https://youtube.com/watch?v=W1uG25of2t0) the agent runs itself.
4. **Wire feedback close to the laptop.** Run those checks in stop hooks so [agents self-correct without a human](https://youtube.com/watch?v=W1uG25of2t0).
5. **Build evals.** Keep a [test suite for every model and prompt change](https://youtube.com/watch?v=-0Ly0cjPdjM).

> Models are interchangeable; the harness around them is the part you actually have to engineer.

## What broke

Grid Dynamics treated production as something to reach, then learned that [staying in production is harder than getting there](https://youtube.com/watch?v=-0Ly0cjPdjM). The fix was not a better prompt — it was moving the undifferentiated reliability plumbing off the application and onto a durable-execution platform. Xavier's parallel fix: stop relying on human reviewers as the feedback loop, and encode their preferences as deterministic guardrails the agent can't skip.

[Harness Engineering](/guide/harness-engineering/)
